1:
The chapter opens on Jehu's cold campaign against the house of Ahab, and the witnesses divide their labour between the arithmetic of the seventy sons and the craft of the letters. The grammatical-historical commentators read 'Ahab had seventy sons' broadly. Charles Ellicott takes the name Ahab as standing for the whole house of Ahab, since the king had been dead about fourteen years and had had two successors on the throne (2 Kings 3:1; 1 Kings 22:51); his posterity in general is meant, though many of the number, given a considerable harem, might be sons in the strict sense. Adam Clarke presses the literal possibility: with several wives Ahab might have many children, and Israel was ever a fruitful stock, so that Rehoboam's thirty-eight sons, Abdon's forty, and Gideon's seventy-one make Ahab's seventy no exaggeration.

All the grammatical-historical voices stumble on one word, 'Jezreel.' Adam Clarke insists it 'certainly should be' the rulers of Samaria, for the whole context shows the letters were sent there (2 Kings 10:6). Charles Ellicott agrees that 'Jezreel' is an ancient error: the Septuagint and Josephus read 'the rulers of Samaria,' and he would restore 'and unto the elders' with several manuscripts, so that the text ran 'sent to the princes of the city and unto the elders.' They note that the nobles who 'brought up Ahab's children' were guardians entrusted with the royal wards, the very custom Isaiah touches when he calls kings 'nursing fathers' (Isaiah 49:23); and Ellicott guesses that Ahab dreaded the intrigues of a harem education and possible disputes about the succession.

The reformed John Gill adds the qualification the historian leaves implicit: these seventy 'might not be all his immediate sons, but some of them his grandsons,' such being sometimes called sons in Scripture.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the moral. Seventy was Gideon's number (Judges 8:30), a quiverful that seemed to secure the family's perpetuity, 'and yet it is extirpated all at once.' Such a quiver of arrows could not protect Ahab's house from divine vengeance; numerous families, if vicious, must not expect to be long prosperous. Henry reads Jehu's opening letter as a taunt: he challenges the well-wishers of Ahab's house to set the best of the sons on the throne and stand by him, not because he wished it but to upbraid their cowardice and 'utter inability to contest with the divine counsels.' The witnesses converge on the theme that will govern the chapter, a doomed dynasty and a shrewd avenger, and behind both the sure word spoken against Ahab that no strength of numbers can annul.
2:
The body of Jehu's letter is a study in irony, and the witnesses are agreed that its show of challenge is really a trap. The grammatical-historical commentators catch the tone exactly. Charles Ellicott renders the opening 'And now when this letter cometh,' noting that only the conclusion of the letter, its very gist, is reported here (compare 2 Kings 5:6); and he hears 'a tone of mocking irony' as Jehu carefully enumerates the nobles' resources, their master's sons, the chariots and horses, the fenced city, and the armoury, as good as saying that his defiance is not the fruit of ignorance. These, he adds, were the same luxurious and cowardly grandees whom Amos would upbraid a few years later (Amos 6:3-6; compare Isaiah 28:1-10).

Adam Clarke reads the whole thing as transparent menace: everything here refers to Samaria alone, where the magazines and implements of war lay, and 'no reader need be told that these letters were all ironical.' It was the same, he says, as if Jehu had written, 'Ye have no means of defense; Israel is with me: if you yield not up yourselves and the city, I will put you all to the sword.' Keil and Delitzsch confirm the effect from the sequel: the ruse had its desired result, for the recipients were in great fear and hastened to announce their submission, protesting that they had no desire to appoint a king.

The reformed John Gill attends to the plain sense of 'your master's sons,' identifying them as the sons of Ahab, with some of Joram, all either the sons or grandsons of Ahab, now shut up in the strong capital. Between them the witnesses expose Jehu's method: he does not march on Samaria but sounds the inclinations of its nobles, dressing a demand for surrender in the language of an invitation to fight. The dare to 'set up a king and fight' is calculated to be refused; and by parading the very strength the city could have used against him, Jehu forces its rulers to measure their courage against a man they already know as a soldier, and to find it wanting. The irony is the weapon, and the terror it breeds does his work for him before a blow is struck.
3:
Jehu's mock summons reaches its point, 'look out the best and meetest of your master's sons, and set him on his father's throne, and fight for your master's house,' and the witnesses read it as a dare designed to be declined. The reformed John Gill takes the words at surface value: the nobles are to choose the son 'most fit for government, whether on account of age or proper qualifications, and wisdom, courage,' and the rest. But this straightforward reading only sharpens the menace underneath, for the invitation to enthrone a rival is issued by the very man sworn to destroy the whole line.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works the phrases with care. 'The best and meetest' means the one they think best qualified in every sense, not merely the morally best. 'Your master,' he observes, need not mean Jehoram, for the story turns on Ahab, so that 'his father's throne' is Ahab's throne, the idiom by which the founder of a line is called the father of his descendants (compare 2 Chronicles 21:12). The clinching clause, 'fight for your master's house,' is where Jehu 'declares his own warlike intentions,' leaving the nobles, whom his prompt and decisive action had taken by surprise, no choice between improvised resistance and instant submission. Knowing Jehu's character as a soldier, they chose the latter.

Here the schools do not divide so much as reinforce one another. Gill supplies the literal sense of the order, and Ellicott supplies its strategy, and together they show that the whole letter is a piece of psychological warfare. Jehu offers the nobles exactly what they cannot take up. To crown a son of Ahab and make war would be to stake their lives on a contest they have already judged hopeless, and Jehu knows it. By framing surrender as the only alternative to a battle he invites them to lose, he manoeuvres the guardians of the royal wards into becoming the executioners of their charge. The verse is thus the hinge on which the nobles turn from protectors of Ahab's house to its destroyers, and the witnesses mark how a single crafted sentence, spoken from a chariot, can topple a dynasty without an army. The next verse will record the fear that the challenge was meant to raise (2 Kings 10:4).
4:
The challenge lands as Jehu intended, and the witnesses fasten on the nobles' collapse of nerve. 'But they were exceedingly afraid, and said, Behold, two kings stood not before him: how then shall we stand?' The reformed John Gill gives the plain psychology: they were 'intimidated at once, for they saw the purport of those letters,' that should they attempt anything of the kind, Jehu would come upon them with his forces. The bravado of the summons had done its work; the guardians of Ahab's house read the threat beneath the invitation and folded.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the Hebrew and finds the fear doubled in the very grammar: the phrase is literally 'And they feared mightily, mightily' (compare Genesis 7:19), and 'the two kings' is emphatic, the article pointing to the two who had just fallen. Those two were Joram of Israel and Ahaziah of Judah, cut down together in Jehu's opening stroke. The logic of the terrified nobles is exact: if two anointed kings, with their armies and their walls, could not stand before this man, what hope had a council of courtiers behind them?

The reasoning here becomes, for the witnesses, more than a note on cowardice; it is an unwitting confession. By owning that Jehu is irresistible, the rulers of Samaria testify to the very providence Jehu will invoke, that the word spoken against the house of Ahab cannot be withstood. Their fear is worldly, a calculation of force against force, and yet it serves the divine sentence, for it hands over the last defenders of the dynasty without a struggle. The witnesses observe the irony without labouring it: men who reckon only with swords and cities have reckoned rightly here, for the human power ranged against them is genuinely overwhelming; but the deeper truth, that heaven had already doomed the house they were set to guard, escapes them entirely. Their prudent submission is at once the shrewdest and the basest course, and the narrative, as Ellicott's chosen word 'butchered' will soon show, does not admire it. The stage is set for the second, deadlier letter (2 Kings 10:6), in which the fear here confessed is turned to Jehu's use.
5:
The surrender is now formal and unanimous, and the witnesses take stock of who bows and how completely. 'He that was over the house, and he that was over the city, the elders also, and the bringers up of the children, sent to Jehu, saying, We are thy servants, and will do all that thou shalt bid us; we will not make any king: do thou that which is good in thine eyes.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the officers precisely. 'He that was over the house' is the prefect of the palace, the major-domo whose position resembled that of a great chamberlain; 'he that was over the city' is its governor, called 'the prince of the city' elsewhere (1 Kings 22:26); and these two are the very 'rulers' named at the head of the chapter (2 Kings 10:1). John Wesley glosses them to the same effect, the chief governor of the king's palace and the chief magistrate or military governor of the city.

Keil and Delitzsch read the message as an unconditional capitulation: the authorities declare themselves Jehu's servants, willing to carry out whatever he commands, with no desire to set up a king of their own. Adam Clarke presses the ominous edge of that blank submission. 'Thus all the constituted authorities agreed to submit,' he writes, and, tellingly, 'they made no conditions, and stood pledged to commit the horrid murders which this most execrable man afterwards commanded.' The whole apparatus of the state, palace, city, elders, and tutors, surrenders its conscience along with its arms.

The reformed John Gill simply fixes the setting, 'the king's palace in Samaria,' over which the first officer had charge. Taken together the witnesses draw a sober portrait of corporate cowardice hardening into complicity. It is one thing to yield to superior force; it is another to write a blank cheque, promising in advance to do 'all that thou shalt bid us,' whatever it may be. Clarke's comment exposes the moral trap: by swearing an unconditional obedience, the nobles have already consented to the atrocity not yet named. The men set to nurse and protect the royal children have pledged themselves to a master who will require those very children's heads, and the next letter will call in the pledge (2 Kings 10:6).
6:
Having gained his point, Jehu presses it home, and the witnesses read the second letter as the moment his cruelty and his shrewdness fuse. The reformed John Gill notes the simple pivot, that 'having gained his point by the former' letter, Jehu now writes again, this time turning the nobles' blank submission into an instrument of slaughter. The demand is that they take the heads of their master's sons and bring them to Jezreel by the same hour the next day.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does not spare the horror of the order. 'Jehu knew his men,' he writes; 'the cool cynicism of his savage order is worthy of a Sulla or a Marius.' He reads the Hebrew 'the heads of the men of the sons of your master,' notes the textual variants, and dwells on the urgency, 'by tomorrow this time,' over a journey of more than twenty miles: Jehu is anxious for despatch because he wishes to convince the people of Jezreel as soon as possible that no royal prince is left to claim the crown, and that the nobles of Samaria have joined his cause. The parenthetic reminder that the king's sons, seventy persons, were being reared by the great men of the city recalls the charge already laid down (2 Kings 10:1); and Ellicott adds that seventy may be a favourite round number rather than an exact count (1 Chronicles). Adam Clarke catches the geographical hint that the phrase 'come to me to Jezreel' lets slip: 'therefore the letters were not written to Jezreel, but from Jezreel to Samaria,' confirming that the address in the first verse was corrupt.

The witnesses converge on the diabolical economy of the plan. Jehu does not soil his own hand with the princes' blood; he compels their guardians to do it, so that the crime is shared and the capital is bound to him by guilt. By demanding the heads within a day, he ensures both terror and speed, presenting Jezreel with an accomplished fact before any counter-move can form. The murder is engineered to look like the act of Samaria's own nobles, which is precisely the impression Jehu will exploit when he stands before the people the next morning (2 Kings 10:9). The witnesses do not admire the craft; they expose it, tracing how a promise of unconditional obedience is turned, within a single letter, into seventy severed heads.
7:
The order is obeyed without a flicker of hesitation, and the witnesses register both the promptness and the horror. The reformed John Gill observes that when the letter came 'they did not in the least hesitate, but immediately complied,' seizing the king's sons, slaying seventy persons, and sending their heads in baskets to Jezreel. Keil and Delitzsch narrate the grim sequence plainly and note what Jehu did with the ghastly delivery, piling the heads in two heaps before the city gate to await the morning.

The wesleyan-arminian voices supply the moral recoil the bare narrative invites. Adam Clarke exclaims at the guardians, 'What cold-blooded wretches were the whole of these people!' John Wesley draws the finer distinction that will run through the chapter: 'Jehu justly required this, because the sovereign Lord of all men's lives commanded it,' yet 'the Samaritans wickedly obeyed it, without any knowledge of, or regard to, God's command.' The same deed, Wesley insists, is righteous as the fulfilment of a divine sentence and wicked as the act of men who obeyed only out of terror and self-interest, caring nothing for the God whose word they unknowingly executed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears the historian's own judgement in his choice of words. 'They took the king's sons, and butchered seventy persons,' he notes; the blunt verb 'butchered' or 'slaughtered' 'shows that the writer did not sympathise with Jehu's deeds of blood.' The narrator's real interest, Ellicott adds, 'centres in the fact that the predictions of Elijah were fulfilled by the wickedness of Jehu' (2 Kings 10:10). Here the witnesses reach a shared and delicate conclusion. Providence and human sin run through the same event without excusing one another: the destruction of Ahab's house was the ordained word of God, but the men who carried it out, both the avenger who devised the plan and the nobles who lent their hands, acted from motives heaven does not commend. Wesley's double reading and Ellicott's unsympathetic 'butchered' hold these truths together, so that the reader is neither allowed to admire the slaughter nor permitted to doubt that God's word stood.
8:
The heads arrive, and Jehu stages them, and the witnesses attend to the theatre of terror he mounts at the city gate. 'There came a messenger, and told him, saying, They have brought the heads of the king's sons. And he said, Lay ye them in two heaps at the entering in of the gate until the morning.' The reformed John Gill supposes the messenger came from the great men of Samaria themselves, to let Jehu know his orders had been obeyed. Adam Clarke reconstructs the timing and the purpose: the heads reached Jezreel in the night, and Jehu ordered them left at the gate, 'a place of public resort,' so that all the people might see them and 'be struck with terror, and conclude that all resistance to such authority and power would be vain.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the scene with his usual detail. The Hebrew is 'and the messenger came in'; Josephus reports that Jehu was giving a banquet at the time; the word for 'heaps' is a rarity found nowhere else in the Old Testament, cognate to a verb meaning 'to heap up' (Exodus 8). The gate, he notes, is 'the place of public business,' where all the citizens would see them (compare 2 Kings 7:3; 1 Kings 22:10), though he wonders whether the palace gate rather than the city gate is meant. To modern eyes the display is barbaric, and Ellicott does not pretend otherwise, comparing both the customs of modern Persia and 'the comparatively recent custom in our own country of fixing up the heads of traitors on London Bridge.'

The witnesses agree on the calculated cruelty of the arrangement, and they read it as of a piece with Jehu's whole method. He does not merely kill; he exhibits, turning the severed heads into a public argument. Two heaps at the gate declare, without a word, that the house of Ahab is finished and that its own capital did the deed. The overnight delay is itself deliberate, letting the citizens gather at dawn to a spectacle designed to break their will before Jehu opens his mouth. The witnesses see in the grisly display the prelude to the speech of self-justification he is preparing, in which the very heaps he has piled will become his evidence that the guilt lies elsewhere (2 Kings 10:9).
9:
In the morning Jehu turns the two heaps into an argument, and the witnesses concentrate on the studied ambiguity of his speech. 'He went out, and stood, and said to all the people, Ye be righteous: behold, I conspired against my master, and slew him: but who slew all these?' Keil and Delitzsch read the manoeuvre exactly: Jehu did not tell the people that the princes had been slain by his command, but spoke as though it had happened 'by a higher decree,' without his interfering, so as to justify his conspiracy in their eyes. Adam Clarke calls the opening word 'another irony,' intended partly to excuse himself and partly to involve the people in the odium of the massacre, while showing that all was done according to the commandment of the Lord.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the scene, Jehu taking his place as judge in the palace gateway after royal custom, the citizens crowding in consternation before 'the two ghastly pyramids.' But he records that the key phrase, 'Ye be righteous,' is genuinely disputed. It may mean 'ye are guiltless in respect of these deaths, and therefore have nothing to dread'; Thenius reads 'ye are just, and therefore will judge justly'; while others render it as a question, 'Are ye righteous?', implying that Jehu wished to make the people share the guilt of the princes' murder while owning only the king's. The following clause, 'but who slew all these?', Ellicott takes as feigned astonishment, a self-exculpation that hints that since Jehovah had foretold the ruin of Ahab's house, Jehovah himself must have brought it to pass.

The reformed John Gill simply pictures the people gathered to the shocking sight. The witnesses agree on the substance beneath the disputed grammar: whatever the precise force of 'Ye be righteous,' Jehu is performing a piece of political theology, spreading the responsibility and cloaking his ambition in the language of prophecy. The speech is at once a confession and an evasion, owning the death of the king while disowning the seventy, and appealing to the divine sentence not from reverence but from self-interest. The next verse will let Jehu name that sentence outright, so that his cunning and God's true word stand side by side (2 Kings 10:10).
10:
Jehu now names the word he has been hinting at, and the witnesses weigh how a true prophecy is pressed into the service of a self-serving speech. 'Know now that there shall fall unto the earth nothing of the word of the Lord, which the Lord spake concerning the house of Ahab: for the Lord hath done that which he spake by his servant Elijah.' The reformed John Gill reads the theology straight: Jehu would have the people observe that all this, and whatever else should follow, was 'brought about by the providence of God, according to divine predictions, and as just punishments on Ahab's family for their sins,' so that neither he nor the nobles were to be blamed, being 'only instruments made use of by the Lord,' who both foretold the judgement and gave orders for its execution.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the picture in the Hebrew. 'Fall unto the earth' means fall 'as a dead thing,' man, bird, or beast (compare Matthew 10:29), so that not one syllable of Elijah's prediction shall fail of accomplishment; and the closing clause is emphatic, 'and Jehovah, He hath done,' or 'and Jehovah it is who hath done' this. The stress falls squarely on the divine agency: what has happened is the fulfilment of a specific, named prophecy, and the doer is God.

Here the witnesses face the chapter's recurring paradox and refuse to resolve it too neatly. The word Jehu quotes is genuinely God's word, and it genuinely stood; Elijah had foretold the extirpation of Ahab's line, and it is being carried out to the letter (compare 1 Kings 21:21-24). Yet the mouth that proclaims it is a politician's, using true doctrine to disclaim responsibility for a slaughter he himself contrived. Gill and Ellicott let both stand: the prophecy is sound, the fulfilment is real, and the speaker is exploiting them. It is possible to speak orthodox truth about providence in order to excuse oneself, and Jehu does exactly that. The witnesses thus teach a double lesson, that God's word never falls to the ground, and that a man may confess as much while his own heart is far from the God he cites, a discrepancy the narrative will state outright before the chapter ends (2 Kings 10:31).
11:
The purge widens from the princes to the whole circle of Ahab's power, and the witnesses mark both its thoroughness and its ruthlessness. 'So Jehu slew all that remained of the house of Ahab in Jezreel, and all his great men, and his kinsfolks, and his priests, until he left him none remaining.' The reformed John Gill reads the verse as the design behind the preceding speech: Jehu's self-justification was aimed precisely at clearing the way for this general slaughter of all who remained of Ahab's house in Jezreel.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parses the several classes. The 'great men' are the high officials of Jehu's fallen predecessor's court, persons who owed their exaltation to that dynasty; the 'kinsfolks' are, more literally, 'his known ones,' his familiars and intimates; and the 'priests' he groups with the domestic chaplains of the royal house (compare 2 Samuel 8:18). Keil and Delitzsch agree that these last were court priests, such as heathen kings kept, not secular counsellors, and note the sweep of the extermination: the more distant relatives, the superior officers of the old regime, its friends and adherents, and its clergy, all fell together, 'until he left him none remaining.'

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke presses the grimmest irony of the verse: 'the great men who had so obsequiously taken off the heads of Ahab's seventy sons fell also a sacrifice to the ambition of this incomparably bad man.' The nobles who bought their safety by murdering their wards did not save themselves; their complicity earned them only a later place among the slain. The witnesses draw the sober conclusion together. On one side stands the sure word of God, which required that Ahab's house be cut off; on the other stands the character of the instrument, a man whom Clarke does not hesitate to call incomparably bad, whose zeal outran his commission and swept up officials, friends, and priests in an indiscriminate slaughter. The completeness of the judgement is not doubted; but the phrase 'until he left him none remaining' is allowed to carry its full weight of horror as well as of fulfilment, and the reader is left to distinguish, as the narrator does, between the justice of the sentence and the ambition of the executioner.
12:
Jehu now turns from Jezreel toward Samaria, and the witnesses pause over the road and the strange place-name where the next bloodshed will fall. The reformed John Gill reads the journey as of a piece with the purge: Jehu goes 'to make a clear riddance there of all that belonged to Ahab, as at Jezreel, and abolish idolatry there.' Keil and Delitzsch attend to the peculiar Hebrew, 'he came and went,' which seems pleonastic but is meant to show that Jehu did not go straight to Samaria but did what follows on the way; and they locate the meeting at 'the binding-house of the shepherds,' where he fell in with the brethren of Ahaziah.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the philology and topography. The place is Beth-eqed-haroim, which the Targum renders 'the shepherds' meeting-house'; the root 'aqad means 'to bind' or 'knot together,' whence the common explanation 'the shepherds' binding-house,' the spot where they bound their sheep for shearing, though he notes that the idea of binding easily passes into that of gathering or meeting. He weighs the identification proposed from Eusebius, a Beithakad fifteen Roman miles from Legio, surviving as Beitkad east of Jenin, but judges it 'too far off the route from Jezreel to Samaria.' Adam Clarke gives the simplest sense of the name, 'probably the place where the shepherds met for the annual sheep shearing.'

The witnesses do not divide here; they cooperate to fix the scene. The detail matters because the narrative is careful to place this next massacre on the road, not in the capital, so that the killing of Ahaziah's kinsmen is distinguished in time and place from the slaughter yet to come in Samaria (2 Kings 10:17). A solitary building among the pastures, a shepherds' rendezvous with its cistern nearby, becomes the unlikely stage for another stroke of Jehu's relentless progress. The very ordinariness of the spot, a place for the humble work of shearing, throws into relief the terror about to unfold there; and the witnesses, by anchoring the event so precisely, prepare the reader for the encounter with the royal visitors from Judah who are about to walk, all unknowing, into the avenger's path (2 Kings 10:13).
13:
On the road Jehu meets a party of Judean princes, and the witnesses explain both who they were and why they walked unwarned into his hands. 'Jehu met with the brethren of Ahaziah king of Judah, and said, Who are ye? And they answered, We are the brethren of Ahaziah; and we go down to salute the children of the king and the children of the queen.' The reformed John Gill clears up the term 'brethren': these were the sons of Ahaziah's brothers, called his brethren as Lot was called Abraham's brother, for his own brothers had already been carried off by the Arabians before he came to the throne.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms it: they are Ahaziah's kinsmen, his brothers in the strict sense having been slain by a marauding troop of Arabs in the lifetime of his father Jehoram (2 Chronicles 21:17; 2 Chronicles 22:1). He renders their answer 'we have come down' to salute, that is, to inquire after the health of, 'the children of the king,' the sons of Joram, and 'the children of the queen,' literally the sons of the gebirah or queen-mother, Jezebel; both classes are reckoned among the 'sons of Ahab' whom Jehu was destroying. News of the taking of Ramoth and of Joram's recovery, Ellicott suggests, may have reached Jerusalem and prompted this visit of pleasure, the princes 'not suspecting the events which had meanwhile happened with the headlong rapidity characteristic of Jehu's action.' Adam Clarke makes the same point about the speed: 'so promptly had Jehu executed all his measures, that even the nearest relatives of the murdered kings had not heard of their death,' and so 'they were all taken as in a net' (compare 2 Chronicles 22:8).

The witnesses draw together a portrait of tragic ignorance. The forty-two are men of Judah, tainted by their kinship and alliance with the house of Ahab, coming cheerfully to pay their respects to relatives already dead. Their courteous errand, a family visit, walks them straight into the avenger's reach at the very moment his sword is unsheathed. The witnesses see in the encounter both the reach of the judgement on Ahab's house, which extended through Athaliah into the royal line of Judah, and the terrible efficiency of Jehu, whose blows fell faster than any warning could travel. The princes' innocent question, 'Who are ye?', answered so frankly, seals their doom in the next verse (2 Kings 10:14).
14:
The meeting ends in slaughter, and the witnesses note the cold order, the number, and the place. 'And he said, Take them alive. And they took them alive, and slew them at the pit of the shearing house, even two and forty men; neither left he any of them.' The reformed John Gill reads the command simply: Jehu bade his guards seize the princes and make them captives before dispatching them. Adam Clarke identifies the grim setting, 'the pit of the shearing house,' probably the place where the sheep were washed before shearing, or their fleeces after, a cistern now turned to a different use.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the motive and the manner. The order 'take them alive' may mean they made some show of resistance; Jehu slew them 'because of their connection with the doomed house of Ahab,' and Keil, he notes, thinks the avenger 'dreaded their conspiring with the partisans of the fallen dynasty in Samaria.' The Hebrew is stark, 'slaughtered them into the cistern of Betheked,' whether by cutting their throats over it or casting the bodies into it. Ellicott adds a curious observation about the tally: the 'two and forty' is 'curiously parallel' with the forty-two youths torn by the bears at Bethel (2 Kings 2:24), and may here be 'a definite for an indefinite number.'

The witnesses do not soften the episode. These were princes of Judah, drawn into Ahab's guilt by the marriage of Jehoram to Athaliah, and their destruction, like the rest, falls within the sentence on that house; yet the narrative's flat, unsparing language, 'took them alive,' 'slew them,' 'neither left he any of them,' invites no admiration for the deed. The witnesses hold the same tension they have traced throughout: the judgement is real and the guilt of the house genuine, but the executioner's relentlessness, killing unwarned travellers at a sheep-washing pit, is recorded without a word of praise. The phrase 'neither left he any of them' echoes the completeness noted at Jezreel, and prepares the meeting with Jehonadab, in whom Jehu will seek a witness of a different kind to his zeal (2 Kings 10:15).
15:
Departing from Betheked, Jehu meets a very different figure, and the witnesses read the encounter with Jehonadab the son of Rechab as a study in searching questions and mixed motives. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the most of it. Jehonadab, though 'mortified to the world,' a man whose posterity would keep his rule against wine and cities for three hundred years (Jeremiah 35:6), came out to encourage Jehu in the work to which God had called him; and 'the countenance of good men is a thing which great men, if they be wise, will value.' Jehu's question, 'Is thine heart right, as my heart is with thy heart?', Henry turns into a probe for every conscience: 'I make a plausible profession, have gained a reputation among men, but is my heart right? Am I sincere and inward with God?' Jehonadab 'gave him his word, and gave him his hand as a pledge of his heart,' and Jehu took him up into the chariot.

The wesleyan-arminian voices sharpen the detail. Adam Clarke reads the exchange as a compact of reformers, Jehu asking whether Jehonadab is his friend in promoting a religious reform, yet he wonders whether 'give me thine hand' means more than a helping hand up into the chariot, by which Jehu 'endeavored to acquire the public esteem,' as if to say his cause must be right, for Jehonadab is with him. John Wesley identifies Jehonadab as a Kenite (1 Chronicles 2:55), 'a man of singular prudence and piety,' come to congratulate Jehu and encourage him to fulfil the will of God, and glosses the question, 'Dost thou heartily approve of me, and my present proceedings.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the background of the Rechabites, a society formed after Elijah's day 'for the active support of the true religion,' whose founder Jonadab retired to the desert, preferring 'the rough life of tents to all the allurements of city life' (compare Jeremiah 35:8-11). It was important to Jehu, Ellicott notes, 'to be seen acting in concert with a man revered for sanctity, and powerful as a leader of the orthodox party.' Herein lies the note the witnesses will not silence. Henry frankly weighs whether Jehu's eagerness to be seen with a saint betrays a heart set on his own advancement rather than on God, warning that 'an upright heart approves itself to God and covets no more than his acceptance,' while those who court 'the applause of men' build on 'a false bottom.' The good man's sincerity is real; the king's use of it remains in question.
16:
Jehu's invitation, 'Come with me, and see my zeal for the Lord,' becomes for the witnesses the clearest window into his character, and they read it with grave suspicion. The reformed John Gill states the misgiving bluntly: the words, offered as Jehu was on his way to destroy idolaters and idolatry, 'seem to savour very much of vain glory, hypocrisy, and a pharisaical spirit.' The zeal is paraded, and the parade itself is the problem.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry develops the charge into a diagnosis of false religion. The saying is 'commonly taken as not well said by Jehu, and as giving cause to suspect that his heart was not right with God in what he did, and that the zeal he pretended for the Lord was really zeal for himself and his own advancement.' Two faults betray him: 'he boasted of it,' as though God and man were mightily indebted to him, and 'he desired it might be seen and taken notice of, like the Pharisees, who did all to be seen of men.' Over against this Henry sets the mark of true grace, that 'an upright heart approves itself to God, and covets no more than his acceptance; if we aim at the applause of men, and make their praise our highest end, we are upon a false bottom.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch shift the ground from motive to the deed itself, and here they are careful to be fair. Judged objectively, they insist, the slaughter of the worshippers of Baal that Jehu was preparing 'was in accordance with the law, and, according to the theocratical principle, was perfectly right,' whatever the mixed and selfish motives that drove it. So the witnesses reach their measured verdict. The act Jehu is about to perform, the extirpation of Baal from Israel, is genuinely commanded and genuinely good; but the man performing it turns even his obedience into a show, summoning a witness to admire his zeal. Henry's contrast between the eye of God and the applause of men frames the whole episode: a right thing may be done in a wrong spirit, and Jehu's readiness to be watched exposes the flaw that the chapter's close will name outright, that his heart was not right with the Lord God of Israel (2 Kings 10:31).
17:
Arriving at last in the capital, Jehu completes the extermination, and the witnesses read the verse as the deliberate closing of the sentence pronounced on Ahab's house. 'And when he came to Samaria, he slew all that remained unto Ahab in Samaria, till he had destroyed him, according to the saying of the Lord, which he spake to Elijah.' The reformed John Gill reads it comprehensively: Jehu 'slew all that were in any connection with him, as related to his family, or as ministers of his in civil or religious things.' No branch of the household, by blood or by office, was left standing.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the Hebrew and the idiom. The verse reads, more literally, 'And he entered Samaria, and smote'; and 'Ahab,' as so often in this chapter, is put for the house or family so called, some manuscripts and the Syriac reading outright 'the house of Ahab' (compare 1 Kings 15:29, where the same total destruction of a doomed dynasty is described). Keil and Delitzsch underscore the theological hinge of the verse, that the slaughter was carried out 'according to the saying of the Lord which he spake to Elijah,' so that the political revolution is presented as the working out of a prophetic word.

The witnesses converge on the meaning the narrator himself stresses. What might look like a usurper consolidating his throne by wiping out every rival is framed, by the closing clause, as the fulfilment of the oracle Elijah received against Ahab after the murder of Naboth (1 Kings 21:21-24). The completeness, 'till he had destroyed him,' answers exactly to the prophetic threat that Ahab's house should perish utterly, and the witnesses will not let the reader forget it. Yet the same voices have already registered, and will register again, that the instrument was a bloody and self-serving man; the fulfilment of God's word does not sanctify Jehu's every motive. Here the emphasis rests on the divine faithfulness: the word spoken years before, in a vineyard, to a solitary prophet, has now been carried to its last letter in the streets of Samaria, and the house that defied heaven is no more.
18:
With the house of Ahab gone, Jehu turns against Baal by a stratagem, and the witnesses divide sharply over whether his opening lie can be excused. 'Ahab served Baal a little; but Jehu shall serve him much.' The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the design without defending it: Jehu 'proclaims a great sacrifice, and that he may do it the more easily, he gathers them all together into one place,' meaning all the while to destroy the worshippers of Baal. His fellow Wesleyan, John Wesley, refuses to let the deceit pass: 'the words being manifestly false, and spoken with a design to deceive, cannot be excused, this being an unmovable principle, that we must not do the least evil, that the greatest good may come.'

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reaches the same verdict from his running comment on the passage: the speech 'rather seems to have been spoken purposely to deceive the worshippers of Baal, and then it cannot be justified,' for 'the truth of God needs not any man's lie.' However good the end, the means is a falsehood, and neither Wesley nor Henry will call it anything else.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers a subtler reading that softens, though it does not erase, the charge. Ahab had in fact served Baal more than a little, as the people well knew, 'but the antithesis was not too strong for Jehu's hidden meaning,' for 'he was thinking of his intended holocaust of human victims' (2 Kings 10:25). On this view the sentence is a grim double entendre rather than a plain lie: Jehu will indeed 'serve' Baal much, by slaughtering his priests at his own altar. The reformed John Gill keeps to the narrative setting, that Jehu 'gathered all the people together,' at least the principal of them. The witnesses thus leave a genuine division. Wesley and Henry judge the words a sinful deception that no good outcome can justify; Ellicott finds in them a savage ambiguity, technically true to Jehu's real intent. Both sides agree on the deadly purpose beneath the proclamation, and the reader is left to weigh whether a trap baited with a double meaning is honesty or falsehood.
19:
The bait is set with a threat that turns the trap's own logic against its victims, and the witnesses expose the guile. 'Now therefore call unto me all the prophets of Baal, all his servants, and all his priests; let none be wanting: for I have a great sacrifice to do to Baal; whosoever shall be wanting, he shall not live. But Jehu did it in subtilty, to the intent that he might destroy the worshippers of Baal.' The reformed John Gill takes the summons to gather every class engaged in Baal's worship, the prophets who invoked him, the priests who sacrificed, and the servants who assisted. Keil and Delitzsch stress the two-edged decree: none is to be absent on pain of death, so that the very penalty meant to ensure attendance ensures that none escape the massacre.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke underlines the irony that the death-threat was framed as devotion: 'whosoever shall be wanting, he shall not live,' because, as Jehu pretended, an absentee would show himself no zealous servant of Baal, 'and all this was done in the very spirit of deceit.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies both the parallel and the philology. The convocation deliberately mirrors Elijah's great gathering of the prophets of Baal on Carmel (1 Kings 18:19), only now to a deadlier end; and the word rendered 'in subtilty' is a rare term connected with the name Jacob, the supplanter, so that the Septuagint renders it literally as a striking with the heel, a tripping-up.

The witnesses read the verse as the mechanism of the whole plot laid bare. Jehu's professed reverence, a great sacrifice at which every worshipper must appear, is precisely calculated to assemble the entire body of Baal's adherents in one enclosed place, where the threat that compels their presence will seal their doom. The narrator's own parenthesis, 'but Jehu did it in subtilty,' spoken plainly to the reader, refuses to leave the deceit in doubt even as the Baalites remain deceived. The schools do not divide here; Gill, Keil, Clarke, and Ellicott alike name the stratagem for what it is, an act of calculated treachery, however real the divine warrant for purging Baal from Israel. The next verses will show the net drawn tight, as the assembly is proclaimed and the temple filled from wall to wall (2 Kings 10:21).
20:
Jehu clothes the plot in the forms of religion, and the witnesses note how piously the trap is dressed. 'And Jehu said, Proclaim a solemn assembly for Baal. And they proclaimed it.' The reformed John Gill explains the term by the analogy of Israel's own worship: such a 'solemn assembly' was like a holy convocation among the Jews, a day on which the people were 'forbidden and restrained from doing any work,' so that all might be at leisure to attend. By decreeing so sacred a festival, Jehu ensures that the whole body of Baal's worshippers will down tools and gather.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the Hebrew a step further: the phrase is better 'Sanctify a solemn meeting' (compare Isaiah 1:13), a word implying that every person who wished to attend would first have to 'sanctify,' or ceremonially purify, himself in due form. The heralds then gave notice of the festival by criers 'through all Israel,' so that the summons ran the length of the kingdom.

Brief as the verse is, the witnesses find in it the cruel perfection of Jehu's design. Every religious solemnity that would normally mark true devotion, the cessation of work, the ceremonial cleansing, the public proclamation, is here bent to gather the devotees of a false god for their destruction. The very scrupulousness demanded of the worshippers, that they purify themselves before approaching Baal, becomes part of the snare, lulling them into the confidence that this is a day of unusual honour for their god. Gill's and Ellicott's notes together show a stratagem that borrows the whole apparatus of sanctity to serve a work of slaughter, so that the more earnestly the Baalites prepare, the more completely they walk into the trap. It is a bitter reversal of the true convocations of Israel, in which the calling of a holy assembly and the sanctifying of the people were the means of grace; here the same forms gather men not to worship but to die, and the piety they demand is a piety turned wholly to deception. The witnesses read in this the characteristic mark of Jehu's whole procedure against Baal, that he clothes a design of destruction in the language and ceremony of devotion, so that the outward show is most sacred at the very point where the inward purpose is most deadly. The narrator lets the irony stand without comment; the assembly proclaimed here will fill the temple of Baal to overflowing in the verse that follows (2 Kings 10:21), and none who obey the summons will leave alive.
21:
The summons succeeds beyond measure, and the witnesses dwell on the terrible completeness of the gathering. 'And Jehu sent through all Israel: and all the worshippers of Baal came, so that there was not a man left that came not. And they came into the house of Baal; and the house of Baal was full from one end to another.' The reformed John Gill notes simply that Jehu 'sent persons to proclaim this solemn assembly' throughout the land, and the whole body of Baal's worshippers answered the call.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works the vivid Hebrew of the last clause. 'Full from one end to another' is 'right as to the sense,' the figure taken from a full vessel, as if we were to say 'the house was brimful'; the rim of a vessel was its 'mouth,' and here the rim of the contents reached the rim of the vessel, the Septuagint rendering it literally 'mouth to mouth.' He also observes a textual curiosity, that the Vatican Septuagint inserts here a note repeating the threat of the earlier verse, an intruded marginal gloss (compare 2 Kings 9:16 for a like insertion), preserving another manuscript's reading of Jehu's proclamation.

The witnesses read the packed temple as the closing of the net, and they do not miss the grim providence in it. The very success of the deception, that 'there was not a man left that came not,' is what makes the coming judgement total: no worshipper of Baal in all Israel absents himself, and so none is spared. The detail that the house was filled 'from one end to another' is not idle; it measures the completeness of the assembly and, by implication, the completeness of the slaughter that will empty it. What the Baalites take for the greatest day of their worship, their god's temple thronged to the walls, is in truth the gathering of the whole cult into a single killing-ground. The witnesses see here the outward triumph of idolatry turned, in a moment, into the instrument of its own extirpation, and the reader, who knows Jehu's real design, watches the crowd press in with a dread the worshippers themselves cannot feel (2 Kings 10:25).
22:
A last precaution marks out the victims, and the witnesses examine both the strange word 'vestry' and the purpose of the vestments. 'And he said unto him that was over the vestry, Bring forth vestments for all the worshippers of Baal. And he brought them forth vestments.' The reformed John Gill takes the officer to be the keeper of the garments 'in which the priests of Baal ministered.' Adam Clarke traces the word to the Latin vestiarium, the place where sacred robes were kept, and notes that the priests of Baal 'had their robes as well as the priests of the Lord,' but garments 'such that one could be easily distinguished from the other.'

The grammatical-historical commentators debate the finer point. Keil and Delitzsch argue that this was the wardrobe of the temple of Baal, not of the royal palace, since the priests of Baal, 'like the priests of almost all religions,' had their own sacred dresses, worn only in worship and kept in a temple wardrobe. Charles Ellicott canvasses the versions, the Targum's 'chests,' the Vulgate's 'those over the vestments,' and inclines to the view that Jehu 'simply gives directions that all the priests and prophets should be careful to wear their distinctive dress at the festival,' rather than that festal robes were distributed from his own palace.

Whichever reading is right, the witnesses agree on the sinister function the vestments serve. By clothing 'all the worshippers of Baal' in their proper robes, Jehu ensures that not one of them can afterward be mistaken for a servant of the Lord, and that the guards will know precisely whom to strike. The provision that looks like an honour, distinctive garments for a great solemnity, is in fact the final mark set upon the doomed, so that the slaughter may be exact and no true worshipper caught up in it by mistake. The care taken to distinguish Baal's robes from Jehovah's, which Clarke notes as a matter of custom, here becomes the means of a lethal sorting. The witnesses thus read the vestry-scene as of a piece with the whole stratagem: every detail of the false worship, down to its very wardrobe, is turned to serve the purge, and the next verse will complete the separation by searching out any servant of the Lord among them (2 Kings 10:23).
23:
Before the blow falls, Jehu clears the temple of any true worshipper, and the witnesses observe how the precaution reads one way to the reader and quite another to the Baalites. 'And Jehu went, and Jehonadab the son of Rechab, into the house of Baal, and said unto the worshippers of Baal, Search, and look that there be here with you none of the servants of the Lord, but the worshippers of Baal only.' The reformed John Gill notes that Jehonadab, going with Jehu into so idolatrous a place, was plainly 'led into the secret' of the design, or he would never have entered. Keil and Delitzsch add the grim staging that accompanies the search: eighty of Jehu's guards were posted outside, each answerable with his own life for any worshipper of Baal who might escape.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supposes the search was made 'on pretext of expelling the profane,' as the heathen themselves did when some special manifestation of their god was expected, so that no suspicion of the real design should be aroused. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out the irony most sharply: 'this precaution of Jehu's suggests suspicion to a modern reader, but it would suggest the very contrary to the Baal-worshippers,' namely 'an extraordinary reverence for Baal, a dread lest some profane person should be present in his sanctuary.' The sorting that we recognize as a death-warrant they welcomed as a mark of unusual zeal.

The witnesses find in the double reading the very genius of the trap. The same act, the careful exclusion of every servant of the Lord, is at once a mercy and a menace: a mercy, in that no faithful worshipper of Jehovah will perish in the coming slaughter, and a menace, in that its true aim is to leave only Baal's own in the net. Jehonadab, the man revered for sanctity, stands beside Jehu as a witness that no innocent blood is being gathered in with the guilty. The precaution that looks pious to the victims is, to the reader who knows Jehu's purpose, the last turn of the screw, ensuring that when the guards enter, every soul in the house is a worshipper of Baal and none other. The command to strike is now only a word away (2 Kings 10:25).
24:
The sacrifices begin, and Jehu sets his guard, and the witnesses read the verse as the moment the trap is armed. 'And when they went in to offer sacrifices and burnt offerings, Jehu appointed fourscore men without, and said, If any of the men whom I have brought into your hands escape, he that letteth him go, his life shall be for the life of him.' The reformed John Gill notes only that the worshippers 'went in to offer sacrifices and burnt offerings to Baal, all things being ready for them,' unaware that their devotions were their own death-service.

The grammatical-historical commentators sort out the staging and the Hebrew. Keil and Delitzsch take the sacrificer to be the officiating priest, not Jehu, and render the king's charge to the runners and aides-de-camp, 'Come and smite them, without one coming out'; the guards were to slay unsparingly. Charles Ellicott adds that the 'sacrifices and burnt offerings' are peace offerings and burnt offerings, offered in the open court, and that the fourscore men had been set 'on the outside' of the building beforehand. The threat, literally 'the man that escapeth of the men whom I am bringing into your hands, his life for his life,' is a little incoherent in the energy of its speech, though Thenius would repoint the verb to read 'the man that letteth escape any of the men,' which some think an improvement.

The witnesses agree on the cold precision of the arrangement. Jehu leaves nothing to chance: eighty picked men ring the temple, and each is made personally answerable, on pain of his own life, for the escape of a single Baalite, so that the guards themselves have every reason to let none slip through. The killing is timed to the worship, to fall when the whole assembly is gathered within and absorbed in its rites, defenceless and unsuspecting. The witnesses do not moralize at length here; the verse is the machinery of the massacre, and they let its chilling exactness speak. The devotions of Baal and the swords of Jehu are poised together, and at the completing of the burnt offering the signal will be given that turns the crowded sanctuary into a slaughterhouse (2 Kings 10:25).
25:
At the close of the offering the signal falls, and the witnesses read the massacre and the storming of the shrine. 'As soon as he had made an end of offering the burnt offering, Jehu said to the guard and to the captains, Go in, and slay them; let none come forth. And they smote them with the edge of the sword; and the guard and the captains cast them out, and went to the city of the house of Baal.' The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke catches the moral irony of the timing: had Jehu been 'a man of any conscientious principle in religion, he would have finished the tragedy before he offered the burnt-offering; but to a man of no religion, the worship of Jehovah and of Baal are alike.' The guard he glosses as the runners, a kind of light infantry, and takes 'the city of the house of Baal' for a sort of inner sanctuary where the chief images were kept.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott suggests the Syriac reading 'when they had made an end,' the priests finishing their sacrifice, and observes that Jehu 'chose the moment when all the assembly was absorbed in worship.' He too explains 'the city of the house of Baal' as the citadel or stronghold of the temple, the guards after their bloody work in the court rushing up into the sanctuary itself, which, like Solomon's temple, 'resembled a fortress.' Keil and Delitzsch read the sequence the same way, the runners and captains smiting the worshippers unsparingly and then pressing on into the inner citadel. John Wesley takes 'the city' for the cluster of buildings belonging to the temple, chambers where the priests of Baal and of the groves lodged (compare 1 Kings 18:19).

The witnesses converge on the horror and its meaning. The slaughter is total, 'let none come forth,' the whole assembly cut down at the very altar where they thought to honour their god; and the guards do not stop at the court but carry the destruction into the innermost stronghold of Baal's worship. Clarke's barb about a man to whom 'Jehovah and Baal are alike' sets the note the chapter will sound at its close: the deed is the Lord's judgement on idolatry, but the hand that performs it is indifferent to true religion, dispatching a burnt offering and a massacre with the same untroubled conscience. The temple that stood full from wall to wall is now to be gutted, its images dragged out and burned (2 Kings 10:26).
26:
With the worshippers dead, the images are destroyed, and the witnesses attend to what exactly was dragged out and burned. 'And they brought forth the images out of the house of Baal, and burned them.' The reformed John Gill takes these to be lesser images, 'the images of other deities, or what were placed as decorations of the temple.' Keil and Delitzsch read the Hebrew more precisely as the 'pillars' or columns fetched out and burned, distinguishing them from the single great 'column of Baal,' the real image of the god, probably a conical stone, which was broken in pieces rather than burned; the wooden columns they take for the symbolic companions of Baal.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the word means 'pillars,' which were of wood and carried a sacred significance (compare Hosea 3:4). He sets them in the wider history of Israel's worship: 'in primitive times a pillar was the distinguishing mark of a holy place,' and idolatrous pillars were commanded to be destroyed (Exodus 23:24), though pillars to Jehovah were long thought allowable, a matter the law would later restrict (Deuteronomy 16:21-22). He notes that the Septuagint has the singular here and the plural in the next verse, and the Syriac the singular 'statue' in both.

The witnesses agree that the destruction is deliberate and symbolic, not merely destructive. To haul the pillars into the open and burn them is to expose the impotence of the god they represented, unable to save his own emblems from the fire; and to reserve the central stone image for a separate breaking underscores that the heart of the cult, not only its trappings, is being demolished. The scholars' care to distinguish the wooden columns burned from the stone pillar broken is not pedantry; it marks the thoroughness of the purge, which spares neither the principal idol nor its attendant symbols. The witnesses see here the practical outworking of the first commandment against a cult that had corrupted Israel since Ahab's day, and the next verse will carry the desecration to its climax in the ruin of the temple itself and its reduction to a thing unclean (2 Kings 10:27).
27:
The desecration reaches its climax, and the witnesses dwell on the deliberate insult heaped upon Baal's sanctuary. 'And they brake down the image of Baal, and brake down the house of Baal, and made it a draught house unto this day.' The reformed John Gill notes the debate over the deity's identity, whether Belus, Saturn, or, as he thinks most probable, the sun, and records the demolition of the temple. Keil and Delitzsch read the final act as calculated contempt: they destroyed the temple itself and made it into privies, the Masoretes softening the coarse word to 'sinks' as a mark of the greatest insult, of which many examples occur among Oriental peoples (compare Ezra 6:11; Daniel 2:5).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the point without euphemism: the 'draught house' was 'a place for human excrement,' a public necessary, so that 'nothing could be more degrading.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes 'the image of Baal' here again as the conical pillar of stone representing the god himself, distinct from the wooden companion-pillars of the preceding verse, and reads the whole as desecration by 'utter' contempt (compare Ezekiel 6:11), the phrase 'unto this day' bearing on the date of the record.

The witnesses see in this final indignity the theology of the whole purge made visible. It was not enough to kill the worshippers and burn the symbols; the very site of Baal's worship must be turned into the filthiest and most contemptible of places, so that the memory of the cult should be not merely erased but made infamous. The god who had claimed the reverence of Israel is answered by the ultimate profanation of his shrine, a lasting monument to his impotence. The scholars' notes on the softened word and the Oriental custom only underline how pointed the insult was meant to be. Thus, the witnesses observe, the outward apparatus of Baal is annihilated root and branch, a triumph the next verse will sum up, even as it prepares the sober qualification that Jehu's own reformation went no further than this (2 Kings 10:29).
28:
The narrator sums up the achievement, and the witnesses at once weigh the deed and probe the motive. 'Thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel.' The reformed John Gill reads the line as comprehensive, Baal's images and temples 'served thus in all places throughout the land of Israel, where there were any.' Keil and Delitzsch mark this verse as the introduction to the history of Jehu's reign, and immediately draw the great qualification that governs the rest of the chapter: Jehu exterminated the worship of Baal, 'but the sins of Jeroboam, the golden calves at Bethel and Dan, that is to say, the idolatrous worship of Jehovah, he allowed to remain.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the motive behind the deed. 'Objectively considered, the slaughter of the servants of Baal was in perfect harmony with the Law; but, subjectively, the motive which influenced Jehu was thoroughly selfish.' The priests and prophets of Baal, depending entirely on the dynasty of Ahab that had introduced the cult, 'might prove dangerous to Jehu; by exterminating them he might hope to secure the whole-hearted allegiance' of the party loyal to the national worship. And the proof of his real principle, Ellicott urges, is his retention of Jeroboam's calves (2 Kings 10:29), which 'proves that he acted from policy rather than religious zeal.'

Here the witnesses face squarely the paradox of Jehu. The thing accomplished was genuinely good and genuinely commanded: Baal, the imported cult that had corrupted Israel since Ahab married Jezebel, is rooted out of the land. Yet the witnesses refuse to credit Jehu with the piety the act might seem to imply, for the same hand that swept away Baal carefully preserved the calves, the state idolatry that served his throne. The distinction is the moral hinge of the passage, and Keil and Ellicott state it plainly: a man may do a work that heaven approves while his heart is set on his own advantage, purging the idolatry that threatens him and keeping the idolatry that profits him. The verse thus stands as both a real triumph over Baal and the opening of an indictment that the following verses will complete.
29:
The verdict on Jehu's half-reformation now falls, and the witnesses probe why a man who broke Baal clung to the calves. 'Howbeit from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin, Jehu departed not from after them, to wit, the golden calves that were in Bethel, and that were in Dan.' The reformed John Gill notes the standing indictment, 'who made Israel to sin,' as 'a blot never to be wiped off,' now fastened upon Jehu as well.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest moral anatomy. Jehu's reformation was partial: 'he departed from the sins of Ahab, but not from the sins of Jeroboam, discarded Baal, but adhered to the calves.' The reason lay in interest. The worship of Baal had made Israel beholden to the Sidonians, and so Jehu 'could easily part with that'; but the worship of the calves 'was a politic idolatry, begun and kept up for reasons of state, to prevent the return of the ten tribes to the house of David, and therefore Jehu clave to that.' Hence Henry's searching rule: 'true conversion is not only from wasteful sins, but from gainful sins, not only from those sins that are destructive to the secular interest, but from those that support and befriend it, in forsaking which is the great trial whether we can deny ourselves and trust God.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the political reading: Jehu 'maintained the worship at Bethel and Dan on the same grounds of state policy' as the kings before him (1 Kings 12:28), and notes that the opening 'Howbeit' is the redactor's constant word, 'Only,' used to qualify his estimate of a king (2 Kings 14:4). John Wesley sums it in a sentence: Jehu's religion 'was but superficial; otherwise it would not have given way to his policy.' The witnesses reach a united and sobering conclusion. Jehu's zeal reached exactly as far as his advantage and no further. He would smash the idol that endangered his crown and cherish the idol that secured it, and in that selective obedience the witnesses read the whole tragedy of a reformation that changes the outward cult without changing the heart, a lesson driven home in the next verse's account of God's measured reward (2 Kings 10:30).
30:
God commends the deed while the man remains flawed, and the witnesses labour to hold both truths at once. 'And the Lord said unto Jehu, Because thou hast done well in executing that which is right in mine eyes, and hast done unto the house of Ahab according to all that was in mine heart, thy children of the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel.' The reformed John Gill supposes the word came by a prophet, Jehu being none himself, and notes the Jewish tradition that it was Jonah the son of Amittai.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the crucial distinction. The divine sentence 'refers to the deed as such, and not to the subjective motives by which Jehu had been actuated,' for it is obvious the act 'had not sprung from pure zeal for the honour of the Lord,' as the very next verse shows (2 Kings 10:31). God approves the thing done, the extirpation of Ahab's godless house, without endorsing the heart that did it. Charles Ellicott adds that the word came 'perhaps through Elisha,' and traces the fulfilment of the promise to the fourth generation (2 Kings 15:12), echoing the commandment's own language of visiting the fathers' sins 'unto the third and fourth generation' (Exodus 20:5).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names the four descendants who fulfilled the promise, Jehoahaz, Jehoash, Jeroboam the second, and Zechariah (2 Kings 15), and observes soberly that this earthly dynasty 'was all the compensation Jehu had in either world, as a recompense of his zeal for the Lord.' Wesley qualifies even the praise, 'done well, in part, and so far as is here expressed.' The witnesses thus reach a carefully balanced judgement. The reward is real and generous, the longest dynasty the northern kingdom would know, and it testifies that no service done for God goes unrequited. Yet it is a temporal reward for a temporal obedience, measured to the deed and not to the man; the God who says 'thou hast done well' of the act will, in the same breath of the narrative, expose the heart that never walked in his law. Approval of the work and indictment of the worker stand side by side, and the witnesses refuse to collapse the one into the other.
31:
The narrator states the flaw that the reward could not cover, and the witnesses read it as the exposure of Jehu's heart. 'But Jehu took no heed to walk in the law of the Lord God of Israel with all his heart: for he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam, which made Israel to sin.' The reformed John Gill draws the line to the inner man: as to his moral conversation, Jehu 'was not careful that it was according to the law of God,' and what he did agreeably to it 'was not sincerely, and from the right principle.'

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is blunter still: Jehu 'never made it his study; indeed, he never intended to walk in this way; it neither suited his disposition nor his politics.' The obedience was never meant to reach the heart or the whole of life. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the verse 'Now Jehu had not been careful to walk in the law,' the Mosaic law that forbade the very images he retained, and explains the qualifying clause 'with all his heart' by what follows: 'he had done honour to Jehovah by extirpating the foreign Baal-worship, but he supported the irregular mode of worshipping Jehovah established by Jeroboam as the state religion of the Northern kingdom.' Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the reason the coming chastisement fell, faithless Israel now ripe for Hazael's rod (2 Kings 10:32).

The witnesses converge on the diagnosis the whole chapter has been building toward. Jehu's failure was not a want of vigour, for no king was more energetic in destroying, but a want of heart. He took no heed; he did not make the law his study; his religion, as Wesley said of it, gave way wherever policy required. The phrase 'with all his heart' is the measure he fell short of, and the witnesses locate his ruin precisely there. A man may be zealous against one idolatry and wedded to another, may earn a dynasty for a deed well done and yet never walk with God, because the whole was calculation rather than devotion. The verse thus seals the portrait and turns the narrative from Jehu's zeal to the judgement his heedlessness invites upon the kingdom.
32:
The consequence of Jehu's heedlessness begins to appear, and the witnesses read the shrinking of Israel as divine chastisement. 'In those days the Lord began to cut Israel short: and Hazael smote them in all the coasts of Israel.' The reformed John Gill notes the providential irony: Israel's dominions were brought 'into a narrower compass' in the days of Jehu, 'though he was so active and courageous, wherefore the hand of God was the more seen in it.' The very vigour of the king made the reverse the plainer testimony to God's displeasure.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke prefers the marginal reading, 'the Lord cut off the ends,' which he explains by Hazael's seizing of the frontier towns and coasts, the beginning of the ravages Elisha had foretold with tears (2 Kings 8:12), soon extending to all the land east of the Jordan. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the loss in its political frame: Jehu, as a vassal and ally of distant Assyria, drew upon himself 'the active hostility of Hazael' of Syria, Israel's nearer and hereditary foe. From the sacred writer's point of view, Ellicott insists, this verse states the direct consequence of Jehu's failure to walk in Jehovah's law with all his heart (2 Kings 10:31); and the phrase 'to cut Israel short' means, literally, to cut off part after part of Israelite territory (Proverbs 26:6).

The witnesses agree that the geography of loss is the map of judgement. The kingdom that had just seen Baal destroyed and its king rewarded now begins to be dismembered from its edges inward, and the narrator ties the two things together: because Jehu took no heed to God's law, God took away Israel's land. The reformation of the cult, real as it was, did not avert the penalty of a heart that never turned; and the witnesses read Hazael's inroads not as mere political misfortune but as the appointed rod, the very stroke Elisha had wept to foresee. The reward to Jehu's house and the chastisement of Jehu's nation are held in a single frame, and the next verse will trace the exact extent of the territory torn away (2 Kings 10:33).
33:
The extent of the loss is now mapped, and the witnesses trace the whole eastern territory torn from Israel. 'From Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead, the Gadites, and the Reubenites, and the Manassites, from Aroer, which is by the river Arnon, even Gilead and Bashan.' The reformed John Gill fixes the region, 'the coast on which Hazael smote them, to the east of the land of Canaan.' Keil and Delitzsch spell out the boundaries, the land of the two and a half tribes from Aroer on the Arnon in the south northward through Gilead, and note that this notice forms the conclusion of the history of Jehu's reign.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the geography and its temper. Aroer, surviving as Ara'ir on the Arnon, was the southern limit of Gilead, which reached northward to Hermon and took in Bashan; the added words 'even Gilead and Bashan' make clear that 'the whole of the land east of the Jordan,' not merely Gilead in the narrower sense, was conquered by Hazael. These conquests, he adds, 'were characterised by great barbarity,' the very cruelty Amos would denounce (Amos 1:3-5) and Elisha had foreseen (2 Kings 8:12). Ewald thinks Hazael seized his moment amid the internal troubles at the outset of the reign, though Ellicott judges that a man of Jehu's energy must soon have restored order at home.

The witnesses read the catalogue of lost provinces as the concrete shape of the divine sentence. The first territory to fall is the trans-Jordan inheritance, the land the tribes of Reuben and Gad had chosen long before for the sake of its pastures; and Matthew Henry, in his summary of the reign, remarks that they now 'smarted for the choice their ancestors made' (Numbers 32). The dismemberment begins where Israel's border met Syria, and it strips away the whole eastern flank at a stroke. The witnesses hold together the two sides of the judgement they have traced from the chapter's close: God kept his promise to Jehu's house and God kept his threat against Jehu's nation, and even the cruelty of Hazael, real and blameworthy in itself, served the sentence that Israel's unfaithfulness had earned (Amos 1:3-4).
34:
The reign is closed with the customary formula, and the witnesses turn it into a meditation on Jehu and on the mystery of God's use of a bad man. 'Now the rest of the acts of Jehu, and all that he did, and all his might, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?' The reformed John Gill notes only that his acts, like those of the kings before him, were recorded in that lost annal. Charles Ellicott adds the textual detail that some authorities omit the 'all' before 'his might,' and that the Septuagint adds 'and the conspiracies which he conspired.'

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes the verse the occasion of his summary verdict, and it is severe. Though Jehu's reign was long, twenty-eight years, 'we know nothing of it but the commencement,' and 'for barbarity and hypocrisy Jehu has few parallels,' as 'the cowardliness and baseness of the nobles of Samaria have seldom been equalled.' Then Clarke raises the chapter's deepest question: 'Ahab's bloody house must be cut off; but did God ever design that it should be done by these means?' His answer distinguishes the decree from the manner. The cutting off of Ahab's family 'was decreed by the Divine justice,' and Jehu's commission was genuinely extensive (2 Kings 9:7); yet 'the means by which it was done, or at least the manner of doing, were not entirely of his appointing.' The men were 'profligate and wicked, and God permitted their iniquity to manifest itself in this way,' so that 'many things seem to be attributed to God as the agent which he does not execute, but only permits to be done.'

The witnesses reach the theological summit the chapter has been climbing. God's justice ordained that Ahab's house should fall, and God's word was fulfilled to the letter; but the cruelty and hypocrisy that marked the execution were Jehu's own, permitted rather than commanded. Clarke's careful line between what God decrees and what he merely allows lets the witnesses affirm the divine faithfulness without laying Jehu's barbarity at God's door. The long reign is passed over almost in silence, its 'might' unrecorded, as if to say that a life which served God's purposes without serving God leaves little worth remembering, a judgement sealed by the bare notice of his death that follows (2 Kings 10:35).
35:
The bloody reign ends peaceably in bed, and the witnesses note the quiet close and its succession. 'And Jehu slept with his fathers: and they buried him in Samaria. And Jehoahaz his son reigned in his stead.' The reformed John Gill observes simply that Jehu 'died as they did,' the common formula for a natural death. Keil and Delitzsch record the burial in Samaria, the succession of Jehoahaz, and the length of the reign given at its close (2 Kings 13:1).

Though the witnesses are few and their notes brief here, the verse carries a weight the chapter has prepared. A man of relentless violence, who had waded through the blood of two royal houses and a temple full of worshippers, is not cut off by the sword he lived by but 'sleeps with his fathers' and is buried in honour in his own capital. The peaceful death is the visible token of the promise given earlier, that for the deed done against Ahab's house his throne should be secure and his sons should reign after him (2 Kings 10:30). His son Jehoahaz succeeds him, the first of the four generations pledged, and the dynasty of Jehu, the longest the northern kingdom would know, begins its course.

Yet the witnesses, reading this notice in the light of the verses just before it, will not let the quiet end be mistaken for a blessed one. The same man who dies in his bed and lies buried in Samaria was the man who took no heed to walk in the law of the Lord with all his heart, and in whose days God had already begun to cut Israel short (2 Kings 10:32). His temporal reward is real, the secure throne and the honoured grave; but it is temporal only, the recompense of a deed and not the seal of a right heart. The witnesses thus read the peaceful death as the exact measure of what Jehu was granted and what he was not: a dynasty on earth, given for a service rendered, and no word here of that walking with God which the narrative has already declared he never learned. Samaria, the capital he made his seat, receives his bones, and the reign passes to a son under whose rule the chastisement already begun would deepen (2 Kings 13:3).
36:
The chapter closes with the length of the reign and the seat of the throne, and the witnesses read even this bare notice for its quiet significance. 'And the time that Jehu reigned over Israel in Samaria was twenty and eight years.' The reformed John Gill marks the distinction of it: this was 'longer than any king of Israel had reigned,' a span no northern monarch before him had matched. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the word order of the Hebrew, which sets 'in Samaria' last, 'perhaps to indicate by emphasis that Jehu made Samaria, and not Jezreel, the seat of his court.'

Keil and Delitzsch, gathering up the close of the reign, explain why the number of years is withheld until the very end, 'contrary to the usual custom,' namely because Jehu's accession to the throne was never formally announced in the narrative; the general character of his reign had been given instead in immediate connection with the account of his anointing and the extermination of Ahab's dynasty. The historian's ordering is thus deliberate, holding back the formal register of the reign until the deeds that defined it had been told.

Brief as it is, the verse lets the witnesses gather the chapter's paradox into a single line. Twenty-eight years, the longest reign Israel's northern kingdom had yet seen, and a settled capital from which the dynasty of Jehu would rule for four generations, are the outward tokens of a promise faithfully kept, the reward pledged for the destruction of the house of Ahab (2 Kings 10:30). And yet the reader who has followed the chapter knows what these figures do not say: that the long and secure reign belonged to a man who took no heed to walk in the law of the Lord, who kept the calves he found convenient, and in whose days Israel had already begun to be cut short (2 Kings 10:31; 2 Kings 10:32). The witnesses leave the two truths side by side, as the narrator does, an outward success measured in years and a capital, and an inward failure that no length of reign could mend. Jehu's twenty-eight years in Samaria stand as the monument at once to God's faithfulness in reward and to the poverty of an obedience that never reached the heart.
